# 🇱🇰 This Week in Sri Lanka

![LastUpdated](https://img.shields.io/badge/last_updated-2026--05--22_06:55:14-green)
[![RSS](https://img.shields.io/badge/RSS-Feed-orange)](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nuuuwan/lk_news_digest/refs/heads/main/rss.xml)
[![Broadsheet](https://img.shields.io/badge/Broadsheet-View-darkred)](https://nuuuwan.github.io/lk_news_digest)

*Generated by gpt-5 from 186 English News Articles published between 2026-05-15 and 2026-05-22.*

## 1. The power regulator estimated that coal supplied to the Norochcholai plant imposed about Rs. 8.5 billion in extra costs and curtailed generation.

Performance data showed <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">higher specific fuel use</span> than under the prior supplier, with unit outputs falling below 300 MW and desuperheating valves hitting 100% opening during operation. <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">Fly‑ash discharge roughly doubled</span> and stack readings for several pollutants rose, though measured values stayed within the Environmental Protection Licence limits.

A <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">three‑week slippage on the tenth cargo</span> and lower net output increased reliance on oil‑fired capacity, raising system costs compared with 2025. The Public Utilities Commission warned that sustained delays or a major unit outage during April–July maintenance windows could tighten evening supply, and a <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">parliamentary subcommittee</span> was set up to review environmental impacts.

## 2. The Supreme Court cleared key counter‑terrorist financing and financial reporting amendments for passage without a referendum.

The court held that the Convention on the Suppression of Terrorist Financing (Amendment) Bill was <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">not inconsistent with the Constitution</span>, allowing it to be passed by a simple majority. For the Financial Transactions Reporting (Amendment) Bill, only two clauses required a <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">special majority</span>, but the Bench indicated that if revised as suggested, those provisions too could pass with a simple vote.

The determinations support alignment with Financial Action Task Force (FATF, an inter‑governmental anti‑money‑laundering standard setter) recommendations after earlier <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">grey‑listing</span> episodes impaired trade finance and remittances. Stronger rules on supervision, reporting and enforcement are intended to lower compliance risk for correspondent banking and cross‑border investment.

## 3. The Cabinet approved a National Regulatory Reform Council chaired by the head of state to cut red tape and improve investment conditions.

The new body will oversee implementation of a time‑bound action plan prepared with <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">Asian Development Bank</span> input and will be serviced by the Industry and Entrepreneurship Development Ministry. The Government cited <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">fragmented permits</span>, unpredictable procedures and SME compliance costs as barriers holding back cash‑constrained firms despite macro stabilisation in 2025.

Sri Lanka aims to lift its competitiveness by simplifying approvals, <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">digitising interfaces</span> and reviewing obsolete rules, moves seen in peer economies that raised Doing Business‑style indicators in prior reform waves. The step follows a decade in which FDI inflows lagged regional averages while rivals expanded preferential access and logistics capacity.

## 4. The Government moved to manage the human–elephant conflict through a legally effective Hambantota managed reserve and a new multi‑agency panel.

Regulations for the <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">Managed Elephant Reserve</span> were published on <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">30 January 2026</span>, enabling a <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">management committee</span>, habitat restoration of six tanks, grassland development and removal of invasives across Sooriyawewa, Lunugamvehera and Thanamalwila. Officials said around 200 animals use the corridor linking three national parks, and solar projects will be <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">barred within the boundary</span> under the new rules.

A separate committee will report on a national framework for prevention, welfare and research, with plans to strengthen conservation functions and develop Pinnawala into a scientific centre. The policy response follows years in which rising encounters increased crop and property losses in dry‑zone districts while spending on barriers and translocations produced mixed results.

## 5. Parliament set up a subcommittee to examine the environmental footprint of the Lakvijaya coal station.

The <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">Sectoral Oversight Committee</span> on Environment, Agriculture and Resource Sustainability constituted the group under MP <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">Bhagya Sri Herath</span> to probe monitoring gaps, assign responsibilities and propose remedies. The move follows rising community concerns over <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">ash handling</span> and air quality alongside reports of increased emissions during recent operations.

Findings are to be tabled for debate, complementing separate reviews of fuel quality and system costs now underway. The oversight step adds scrutiny to the country’s largest thermal asset, which supplies roughly <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">a third of grid energy</span> in a typical year.

## 6. Authorities kept piped‑water tariffs unchanged for the first half of 2026 under the 2024 formula.

A National Water Supply and Drainage Board review found the current schedule covers <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">operating expenses</span> without requiring a mid‑year upward revision. The <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">semi‑annual approach</span>, adopted in July 2024, links charges to input costs and efficiency metrics to avoid ad hoc adjustments that complicate household budgeting.

Holding rates steady provides limited relief as other utilities have faced fuel‑driven cost pressure; <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">inflation rose to 5.4% in April</span> from 2.2% in March on energy pass‑through. Water pricing will be reassessed again mid‑year based on the formula triggers.

## 7. Colombo sought consular access and safe return after a Sri Lankan medic from a Gaza‑bound flotilla was detained by Israeli forces.

The Foreign Affairs, Foreign Employment and Tourism Ministry said it was <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">engaging via diplomatic channels</span> and expressed distress over footage of detainees circulated online. It added that arrangements were being made to ensure the individual’s <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">welfare and repatriation</span>, noting that thousands of Sri Lankans work across the region.

Subsequent reports indicated deportation proceedings via a Turkish transit. The episode drew international comment as governments protested treatment of activists intercepted in <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">international waters</span>.

## 8. The Cabinet backed direct potato purchases from smallholders via the CWE to stabilise prices in the main harvest.

The scheme will route stocks to <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">Lanka Sathosa</span> outlets, with a ceiling of <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">1,000 kg per farmer</span> to spread the benefit. Reported grower rates at the season’s start ranged between <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">Rs. 200 and Rs. 220 per kilogram</span>, and authorities warned of further softening as volumes peak in Nuwara Eliya and Badulla.

A <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">special commodity levy of Rs. 80</span> per kilogram remains on imports to support domestic producers who face higher input costs than regional competitors. The intervention is intended to smooth volatility while preserving consumer access through public retail.

## 9. SriLankan Airlines disclosed two overseas fraud incidents involving diverted vendor funds and suspected embezzlement.

A payment of <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">AED 974,000</span> to a service provider in the UAE was misdirected after <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">email credentials were reportedly compromised</span> and bank details altered in official‑looking correspondence. Separately, about <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">INR 22 million</span> in Chennai was allegedly siphoned by finance staff through invoice tampering, prompting police complaints in India.

The carrier said it alerted the Sri Lankan Criminal Investigation Department and foreign authorities and suspended further transfers pending the probes. The cases add to sector‑wide concerns about <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">business‑email compromise</span> and internal control lapses in cross‑border operations.

## 10. Sri Lanka and Belarus signed an air services agreement and MoUs on higher education and health during an official visit.

Talks in Minsk also covered plans to convene the <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">Inter‑Governmental Commission</span> on Trade and Economic Cooperation and a third round of political consultations within the year. The two sides discussed tourism flows, potential <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">direct flights</span> and export opportunities for tea, spices, apparel and value‑added goods.

The engagements included meetings with the Prime Minister and parliamentary leadership, and follow‑up activity will focus on student exchanges and clinical collaboration. The outreach broadens Colombo’s bilateral portfolio as it seeks <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">non‑traditional markets</span>.
